risks.risk_title: Cyber Attacks on Vestas' Digital Infrastructure
risks.description: Potential cyber incidents disrupting power production, outages, and financial losses due to targeted attacks on digitally connected wind turbines and operational systems. Threat actors seek to trigger power outages, impacting customers and society.
mitigation: Strategic cyber risk management (safeguarding Vestas’ strategic objectives)
Customer cyber risk management (protecting energy solutions delivered to customers)
Manufacturing cyber risk management (securing manufacturing facilities)
Operational cyber risk management (managing risks across digital systems and services)
Built-in security standards (ISA/IEC 62443 Series compliance for all technologies)
Internal Security Operations Centre (real-time monitoring of global digital infrastructure)
Customer-focused Security Operations Centre (monitoring serviced customer power plants)
Cyber Academy (employee training to reduce attack surfaces and detect threats)
Contribution to open-source initiatives for standardizing cyber risk quantification
Engagement with scientific bodies for threat intelligence, economics, and cyberattack path analysis

risks.description: Exposure to risks arising from a rapidly evolving renewable energy sector due to technological and market changes.
Global business operations across diverse markets introduce operational challenges requiring structured risk management.
Financial and strategic risks stemming from global economic fluctuations affecting short- to medium-term objectives.
Potential regulatory hurdles impacting compliance and business strategy across global markets.
Material risks including climate change mitigation and responsible business conduct affecting strategic goals.
mitigation: Review by Executive Management Team, Board of Directors, Audit Committee every six months

risks.description: Conflicts in Ukraine and Middle East, intensified trade barriers, and assertive industrial policies (EU/China/USA) disrupt supply chains, increase costs, and weaken project economics.

risks.risk_title: Project execution delays and quality issues
risks.description: Complex offshore projects face coordination challenges, scaling difficulties, and logistical disruptions leading to delays, cost overruns, and reputational damage.
mitigation: Continuous alignment between commercial and delivery teams

risks.description: Evolving cyber actors target Vestas’ digital assets, critical infrastructure, and energy solutions for extortion or destabilization, risking economic loss and reputational harm.
mitigation: Supplier coordination for timely component availability

risks.description: GHG emissions from Vestas’ operations and value chain pose negative climate impacts, while low-emission materials offer financial opportunities.
mitigation: Offering low-emission materials improves value proposition

risks.description: Increased carbon taxes and tariffs raise costs for GHG-intensive materials like steel, impacting wind turbine production.
Fossil energy use in manufacturing and service of turbines negatively impacts climate, though scored as immaterial for financial materiality.
Negative impact on biodiversity and ecosystems due to GHG emissions from raw material extraction, production, and transportation.
Landfill or incineration of non-recyclable blade materials increases resource outflow and environmental impact.
mitigation: Offering blade circularity solutions improves value proposition

risks.risk_title: Resource inflow risks in upstream supply chain
risks.description: Climate and environmental impacts from extraction/production of metals and raw materials.
Actual health and safety incidents among employees cause delays, lost work hours, and direct compensation costs.
Upstream workers' health safety incidents are deemed immaterial but contractors/sub-contractors pose risks with injuries leading to delays and compensation costs.
Potential forced or child labor in the value chain carries fines and reputational risks due to Vestas’ global supplier network.

risks.risk_title: Land-use restrictions during wind farm construction
risks.description: Temporary negative social and cultural rights impact due to displacement (economic loss) from land-use restrictions during wind farm construction.

risks.risk_title: Failure to respect indigenous peoples' rights
risks.description: Negative impact on indigenous communities due to violation of their right to own, control, and use lands in wind farm development.

risks.risk_title: Insufficient market conditions for wind deployment
risks.description: Adequate grid expansion, effective auction design, and permit delays could increase costs, hinder investments, and reduce viability of wind energy projects.

risks.risk_title: Cyber attacks on wind farm infrastructure
risks.description: Potential cyber security incidents disrupting operations, exposing intellectual property, causing power outages, and increasing electricity prices.

risks.risk_title: Risk of corruption and bribery exposure
risks.description: Vestas’ global presence increases exposure to corruption and bribery, despite being scored as immaterial.
High carbon taxes on steel and iron will increase material costs for wind turbines, raising overall turbine expenses.
Failure to meet Scope 1/2/3 GHG emission targets (e.g., decarbonizing steel suppliers) could lead to financial penalties or reputational damage.
Slow adoption of low-carbon alternatives (e.g., steel) may force Vestas to absorb higher costs, impacting profitability and competitiveness.
Unforeseen political shifts (e.g., carbon tariffs) could disrupt market stability and increase operational costs.
Increased energy use in manufacturing/construction may strain operations and emissions, despite industry growth.
Reliance on fossil fuels (e.g., Scope 1) and grid electricity (Scope 2) exposes Vestas to rising emission costs.
Rapid expansion of wind energy may create supply chain bottlenecks or price fluctuations in components.
Increased use of sustainability criteria in tenders could exclude Vestas if emissions targets aren’t met.
mitigation: Enabling businesses/society to reduce GHG emissions

risks.description: Over-reliance on fossil fuels for operations may lead to supply shortages and higher costs.
Significant investments in renewable energy and carbon tax mitigation are required, with immature commercial solutions increasing alignment costs.
mitigation: EUR 32.4m (2024: EUR 23.2m) OPEX for carbon tax mitigation

risks.description: Immature commercial or technical alternatives delay full adoption of renewable energy and emissions reduction levers.

risks.risk_title: Slow EV fleet transition in service operations
risks.description: Only 25% (down from 26%) of vehicles are renewably fuelled by end-2025, despite targets for regional EV adoption.
mitigation: Increased charging infrastructure (160+ chargers in Denmark)

risks.description: Scope 3 GHG target of 45% per MWh by 2030 requires supplier collaboration; progress depends on market-based solutions.

risks.risk_title: Climate policy uncertainty affecting global targets
risks.description: Industry may struggle to meet climate goals without supportive policies, risking operational alignment delays.
Pilot vessel (2025–2026) relies on evolving methanol standards; operational updates may extend timeline.
mitigation: 10-year charter agreement with Northern Offshore Service

risks.risk_title: Renewable energy sourcing dependency on policies
risks.description: 100% renewable electricity sourcing depends on supportive policies; lack of them risks operational disruptions.
Future climate risks (e.g., water stress, extreme rainfall) are based on uncertain assumptions; physical impacts may not materialize financially.
mitigation: Value-chain prioritization for direct impact areas
